name: Riotinto Mines and Peña del Hierro Loop
name_original: Minas de Riotinto, Peña del Hierro, Nerva – ruta circular
description: This moderate 20.8-mile mountain biking route, the Minas de Riotinto, Peña del Hierro, Nerva circular, explores a unique Martian landscape.
region_intro: Mountain bike trails around Minas De Riotinto traverse a unique landscape shaped by centuries of mining, featuring dramatically altered terrain often described as lunar or Martian. The region is characterized by the vibrant reddish Río Tinto, colossal open-pit mines like Corta Atalaya, and surrounding Mediterranean mountains and scrubland. These distinct geological features create a diverse environment for mountain biking, from former mining tracks to natural trails. The area offers a network of routes that explore both the industrial heritage and the natural beauty of the Huelva province. Best mountain bike trails around Minas De Riotinto The most popular mountain bike trail is Riotinto Mines and Peña del Hierro Loop, a 20.8 miles (33.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 50 minutes to complete. This moderate route offers views of the historic mining operations and the unique red river. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Corta Atalaya Mine – Embalse del Gossan loop from Nerva, a difficult 49.2 miles (79.2 km) path. This challenging route explores the immense Corta Atalaya open-pit mine and the scenic Embalse del Gossan reservoir. Local mountain bikers also love the Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Embalse del Gossan loop from Zarandas-Naya, a 29.4 miles (47.4 km) trail leading through the historic mining basin and past the Embalse del Gossan, often completed in about 4 hours 13 minutes. Mountain biking around Minas De Riotinto is defined by dramatically altered mining landscapes, the vibrant reddish Río Tinto, and Mediterranean mountains and scrubland. The network offers options for easy, moderate, and difficult routes, catering to various ability levels. The routes in Minas De Riotinto are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.3 stars from more than 10 reviews. More than 200 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Minas De Riotinto's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available in Minas De Riotinto?
A: There are over 30 mountain bike trails around Minas De Riotinto, offering a diverse range of experiences. These include 4 easy routes, 22 moderate routes, and 5 difficult routes, catering to various skill levels.

Q: Are there easy mountain bike trails suitable for beginners in Minas De Riotinto?
A: Yes, Minas De Riotinto offers 4 easy mountain bike trails. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e995549437/riotinto-mines-and-pena-del-hierro-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Riotinto Mines and Peña del Hierro Loop&lt;/a&gt;, while moderate, provides a good introduction to the area's unique landscapes. Additionally, the Vía Verde de Riotinto, built on old mining tracks, offers a generally accessible and continuous path suitable for less experienced riders.

Q: What are some challenging mountain bike routes for experienced riders in Minas De Riotinto?
A: For experienced riders seeking a challenge, there are 5 difficult mountain bike routes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24522907" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine – Embalse del Gossan loop from Nerva&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 49.2-mile (79.2 km) path that explores the immense Corta Atalaya open-pit mine and the scenic Embalse del Gossan reservoir, featuring significant elevation gains.

Q: What unique landscapes can I expect to see while mountain biking in Minas De Riotinto?
A: Mountain biking in Minas De Riotinto offers an extraordinary experience through a landscape often described as 'lunar' or 'Martian.' You'll encounter the vibrant reddish Río Tinto, colossal open-pit mines like Corta Atalaya, and the surrounding Mediterranean mountains and scrubland, all shaped by centuries of mining activity.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in the area?
A: Yes, many of the mountain bike routes in Minas De Riotinto are circular. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/27625689" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Embalse del Gossan loop from Zarandas-Naya&lt;/a&gt; is a 29.4-mile (47.4 km) circular trail that takes you through the historic mining basin and past the Embalse del Gossan.

Q: What are the best times of year for mountain biking in Minas De Riotinto?
A: The best seasons for mountain biking in Minas De Riotinto are typically spring and autumn. During these times, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the potentially wetter conditions of winter. This allows for more comfortable exploration of the region's unique landscapes.

Q: What do other mountain bikers enjoy most about the trails in Minas De Riotinto?
A: The mountain bike trails in Minas De Riotinto are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.3 stars from over 10 reviews. Reviewers often praise the unique 'lunar' landscapes, the historical mining sites, and the diverse terrain that offers both challenging climbs and scenic descents.

Q: Are there any notable viewpoints or attractions along the mountain bike trails?
A: Yes, the trails often pass by significant landmarks. You can see the immense &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5287308" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine&lt;/a&gt;, the striking &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5300427" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Peña del Hierro&lt;/a&gt;, and the unique &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4402997" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Source of the Río Tinto&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also offer panoramic views of the dramatically altered mining landscape and the vibrant red river.

Q: Can I find routes that explore the historical mining infrastructure?
A: Absolutely. The region's mountain bike routes are deeply intertwined with its mining history. Trails often follow old railway lines, like the Vía Verde de Riotinto, and pass by former mining operations, including the colossal open-pit mines and historical stations, providing a unique blend of nature and industrial heritage.

Q: What is the longest mountain bike trail available in Minas De Riotinto?
A: One of the longest mountain bike trails is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/24522907" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Corta Atalaya Mine – Embalse del Gossan loop from Nerva&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 49.2 miles (79.2 km). This difficult route offers an extensive exploration of the region's mining landscapes and natural features.

Q: Are there mountain bike trails that pass by water features or reservoirs?
A: Yes, several routes incorporate water features. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31439495" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Agrio River – Widest Area – Abandoned Tunnel loop from Nerva&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, follows the Agrio River. Additionally, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/27625689" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Old Zarandas-Naya Station – Embalse del Gossan loop from Zarandas-Naya&lt;/a&gt; pass by the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6268303" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Gossán Reservoir&lt;/a&gt;.
tips: It is one of the several buildings of the Riotinto Museum that is distributed in several of the old mines within what is known as the Riotinto Mining Park. More information can be obtained on the official website https://parquemineroderiotinto.es/

It is undoubtedly the most impressive of all the mines in the Riotinto Park. It knew how to be a world reference in this activity and is still one of the symbols of Huelva, today also recognized for its tourist character, attracting visitors from all over the world. Guided tours can be done, I suggest you plan by consulting the official website:
https://parquemineroderiotinto.es/

A must visit if you are in the area. It is advisable to book to see all the centers: the museum, the Peña del Hierro mine, the railway, the Atalaya short mine and Casa 21. The contrasts of copper colors and, in some places, the smell of sulfur make it unique.

This road that runs next to the old Riotinto Mines will give you the impression that you are traveling through another planet. A very peculiar landscape and different from what you may know, there is not too much green in the vicinity but instead you will have a varied palette of colors ranging from ocher, gold, yellow, orange and gray.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Minas de Riotinto, Peña del Hierro, Nerva circular mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: This circular mountain bike route is approximately 33.5 km long and generally takes around 2 hours and 50 minutes to complete. However, your actual time may vary depending on your fitness level, the number of stops you make to enjoy the unique scenery, and the technical sections of the trail.

faqs.question: What is the starting point for this mountain bike route and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The route is circular, so you can start from various points. A common and convenient starting point would be near the towns of Minas de Riotinto or Nerva, where you can find parking. Specific parking details are not provided, but generally, parking is available in or near the towns that serve as access points to the Parque Minero de Riotinto.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this mountain bike route?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this route is quite varied and challenging. You'll encounter a mix of tracks, paths, and singletracks, often winding through pine forests and areas covered in cistus. Expect varied surfaces, including rocky sections, former railway trenches, and potentially muddy or watery areas, especially near the Río Tinto. There are also some significant descents described as 'brutal' and 'pretty tough climb[s]', indicating technical elements and considerable elevation changes.

faqs.question: What historical and natural landmarks will I see on the route?
faqs.answer: This route is rich in both historical and natural landmarks. You'll experience the 'lunar' or 'Martian' landscape created by centuries of mining. Key highlights include the Riotinto Mining Museum, the impressive open-pit Corta Atalaya Mine, and the Peña del Hierro Mine. You'll also ride along sections of the Greenway from Peña de Hierro to Pozo Rotilio and witness the uniquely colored, acidic waters of the Río Tinto.

faqs.question: When is the best time of year to ride this mountain bike trail?
faqs.answer: The best time to ride this trail is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November) months. During these seasons, the temperatures are milder and more pleasant for mountain biking. Summers can be very hot, and winters might bring more rain, making some sections muddy. Checking local weather conditions before your ride is always recommended.

faqs.question: Is this mountain bike route dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While specific regulations for dogs on this exact route are not detailed, generally, dogs are permitted on trails in natural areas like this, especially if kept on a leash. However, given the technical terrain, potential for rocky or muddy sections, and the presence of mining sites, consider if the route is suitable for your dog's fitness and paw safety. Always ensure your dog is under control and that you clean up after them.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for mountain biking in the Parque Minero de Riotinto area?
faqs.answer: The route passes through the historic mining landscape of the Parque Minero de Riotinto. While access to the general trails for mountain biking typically does not require a specific permit, some attractions within the park, such as the Riotinto Mining Museum or specific mine tours, do have entrance fees. It's advisable to check the official Parque Minero de Riotinto website for the latest information on any fees or regulations for specific areas you plan to visit.

faqs.question: Does this mountain bike route connect with other trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this circular route incorporates sections of several other trails. You will find yourself on parts of the Camino de Santiago del Sur, the Sendero miradores Peña del Hierro, and the Vía verde Peña del Hierro a Pozo Rotilio. Other smaller sections include Camino Local a la Peña de Hierro and sendero nacimiento del río tinto a peña del hierro, offering a varied experience.

faqs.question: Is this route suitable for beginner mountain bikers?
faqs.answer: This route is rated as moderate in difficulty. While not extreme, it includes 'pretty tough climb[s]' and 'brutal' descents, along with varied terrain like rocky sections and potential undergrowth. It's best suited for mountain bikers with a good level of fitness and some experience with technical trails. Beginners might find certain sections challenging.

faqs.question: What should I pack for mountain biking this trail?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length and moderate difficulty, you should pack essentials like plenty of water, energy snacks, a basic bike repair kit (spare tube, pump, multi-tool), and a first-aid kit. Wearing appropriate mountain biking gear, including a helmet, gloves, and comfortable clothing, is crucial. Sun protection (sunscreen, sunglasses) is also recommended, especially during warmer months, due to exposed sections of the trail.

faqs.question: Are there places to eat or drink near the trail?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route passes near the towns of Minas de Riotinto and Nerva. Both towns offer options for cafes, pubs, and restaurants where you can refuel before or after your ride. You can also find accommodation options in these towns if you plan an overnight stay.

waypoints.name: Greenway from Peña de Hierro to Pozo Rotilio
